dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 36 at Apt #4 in Richmond-priority two for chest pain.
Ma'am, I'm Charlie with Rescue Squad 85. What made you call 911 today?
My right shoulder and chest started hurting about 30 minutes ago when I was watching TV.
Can you tell me who you are and where we are?
I'm Margot Reynolds. I'm at home in Richmond.
Are you aware of the time?
It's late at night.
Can you squeeze my finger and tell me both are the same?
Yes, they feel the same.
Airway is patent, and you're speaking clearly. Any trouble breathing?
Let me check your breathing. No signs of a blockage.
Are you able to breathe easily?
Pulse is strong at 61. Normal skin tone-let me take your blood pressure.
Heart rate of 61, steady and strong.
Respiratory rate is 20, regular.
Blood pressure is 115 over 72 now.
Pulse ox is 98 percent-good oxygen levels.
We understand you've had stroke and CHF in the past. Has the pain gotten any worse over time?
It began while watching T.V., and the pain has stayed steady.
What kind of feel is this pain-burning, sharp, dull, or pressure?
It's a throbbing pain, hard to shake.
Are you currently taking any medications?
Has the pain moved to any other parts of your body?
I take Carteolol, and my right shoulder pain has moved to the left now.
What's the last thing you ate before the pain started?
I had a sandwich around the time the pain started.
Let me test your blood sugar now. One moment.
Setting up the 4-lead EKG. We'll also run a 12-lead for better results.
I'm placing a 20-gauge IV in your left arm for fluid monitoring. Please let me know if it's painful.
Field impression-cardiac-related chest pain. Activating Protocol 2-1.
Repeat respiratory rate is now 19, usually even on both sides.
Pulse is remaining steady at 66 bpm, consistent and firm.
Blood pressure has dropped slightly to 112 over 73.
Pulse ox shows a decrease to 74. We'll be keeping track of this closely.
Your medical history-including stroke and CHF-has been documented. Are there any other concerns to note?
How about the pain-still an 8 out of 10?
Yes, no reduction in pain level.
Skin looks pink, warm, and dry. Did you feel any sweating earlier?
I had some sweating at the beginning but not now.
No signs of redness, rash, or bruising. Again, skin looks normal.
Transporting to Retreat Hospital. You'll be positioned in semi-Fowler's for ease.
Patient needs no special precautions for movement at this time.
We've also prepared all transport documentation for your medical records.
Devices and harnesses are ready for transport. Double-checking now for safety.
ED Bed 8, updating hospital on the patient's current condition.
Initial EKG showed sinus bradycardia and later transitioned to sinus rhythm.
ASA was given pre-arrival at 324 mg. IV and EKG monitoring in place.
Blood sugar was 167 initially, then showed a read of 0 with a current ox of 74.
Patient remains stable aside from low oxygen levels. No stroke indicators at this stage.
GCS score at 15 with awareness of time, person, place, and event at handover.
Skin initially showed some clamminess but has since returned to normal pink and dry.
Elbows and extremities showed no irregularity-equal strength and no asymmetry.
Patient ambulated to the stretcher independently without difficulty.
No unusual sounds in the chest cavity. No shortness of breath reported.
Vital signs are stable with the exception of oxygen, which is currently at 74.
All monitoring is operational and routinely tracked. No further actions noted.
All interventions followed standard protocol-IV in place and EKG running.
All harnesses secured during transport. No mobility concerns observed.
Patient transportation will be cold to preserve condition and comfort.
Patient has a medical history of stroke from clot and CHF-note history for hospital.
Transport included a non-emergent approach; no movement hazards reported.
Pain remains 8/10. No known allergic reactions or medications besides Carteolol.
No emergency protocols engaged. Movement under full control and observation.
All safety checks noted and in compliance. No transport issues in process.
Patient remains alert and cooperative. No abnormalities or grip weaknesses noted.
Please observe the lower pulse ox readings and maintain monitoring post-handover.
EKG, IV, vitals, and interventions complete. Patient calmed during transport.
All documentation updated in real-time, complete for hospital and medical team.
12-lead EKG was stable with no acute anomalies-no imminent cardiac concern.
No transport complications observed. Protocol was followed completely.
Hospitals aware and ready for care upon arrival. No adjustments required.
Handover complete. Full care now under hospital medical staff supervision.
Confirming all harnesses are fastened correctly for safety.
No abnormalities recorded during the movement phase.
Any specific advice for the receiving hospital staff?
Key points: low ox, IV access, normal strength, and GCS remains at 15.
Device and documentation reviewed and updated.
Transport equipment and findings consistent from start to finish.
All systems operational. No disruptions recorded during trip.
Left IV stable with no flow issues reported.
Pain level remained constant with no relief from medication or movement.
Equal grip and function in both hands reported at all times.
Earlier sweat symptoms normalized with no recurrence of clamminess.
All oxygen and IV equipment functioned as expected.
Documented all readings and change notes for hospital records.
Full care under monitoring from EMT and Paramedic throughout journey.
Receiving hospital will be given final note and control for admission.
All hospital information shared and confirmed preparedness.
Full compliance with emergency actions from start.
We noted only a minor drop in oxygen levels.
Vital signs remained consistent throughout the emergency process.
All steps were accounted for in logs and emergency records.
Patient remained mobile with no sudden deterioration seen.
Full status ready for handover with no actions left.
Final checks complete-control transitioning to hospital staff.
IV secure, all limbs mobile. Equipment and paper ready for signover.
